title: Shell pattern marbling
subtitle: Drawing a combed nonpareil into a bouquet pattern on carrageenan size
excerpt: The shell pattern is one of the most recognisable in Western marbling: a nonpareil base combed into curved bouquets by drawing a pointed stylus in vertical arcs through the colour bands. The result is a scalloped pattern that covers an entire sheet evenly.
sourceNotes: Charles W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881), chapter on combed patterns including the nonpareil and shell. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. Woolnough describes the shell as a secondary pattern derived from combing a nonpareil base.

glossaryTerms.definition: A base marbling pattern created by drawing a fine comb through alternating colour bands laid across the tray. The comb's teeth drag the colours into a series of tight parallel waves. Nonpareil is both a complete pattern in its own right and the starting point for shell, bouquet, and peacock patterns.

A marbling pattern derived from a nonpareil base by drawing a pointed stylus in alternating upward and downward arcs through the combed waves. The arcs pull the wave crests into scalloped curves that resemble a row of overlapping shells.

The thickened water surface on which marbling pigments float. Carrageenan seaweed extract is the modern standard; historically gum tragacanth was used. The size holds the pigments in place during combing and allows them to transfer to the paper in a single contact.

content.text: Shell pattern marbling is a two-stage comb technique. The first stage lays colour bands and combs them into a
nonpareil
. The second stage draws a stylus through the nonpareil in curved arcs to pull the crests into the characteristic scalloped
shell
shapes. Both stages must be completed before the pigments begin to bleed together; working time on carrageenan
size
is approximately two to three minutes before definition begins to soften.
What you need
You also need: a stirring spoon; newspaper strips for skimming the size between prints; and at least four contrasting colours.
Preparing the size and colours

Mix carrageenan at 1 to 1.5 teaspoons per litre of warm water. Blend in a liquidiser or immersion blender to dissolve fully, then leave overnight for air bubbles to dissipate. The size should be the consistency of light syrup.

Thin each acrylic colour with water until it flows freely from a spoon. Add ox-gall one drop at a time, stirring and testing by dripping the colour onto the size surface. The drop should spread to a circle roughly 5 to 8 cm across. Too little ox-gall: the drop beads and refuses to spread. Too much: the drop spreads so thin the colour barely registers.
Stage one: laying and combing the nonpareil base
Skim the size surface with a newspaper strip to remove dust and any skin that has formed.

Drop alternating colours across the surface in parallel rows, using the eyedropper. The drops will spread and merge into adjacent bands. Aim for four to six distinct colour bands running the width of the tray. Work quickly: you have approximately three minutes total.
Draw the marbling comb through the colour bands from one short end of the tray to the other. The comb teeth drag the bands into parallel waves. This is the nonpareil base.

Optionally, run the comb back in the opposite direction to create a double-combed nonpareil with finer waves. Do this only if your colour bands are evenly laid; uneven bands will break into fragments.
Stage two: drawing the shell arcs

Take the marbling stylus. Starting at one long edge of the tray, draw it in a smooth upward arc across the nonpareil, approximately 3 to 5 cm wide at its peak. This pulls the wave crests upward, creating the top of the first shell.

Move the stylus 3 cm along the long edge and draw a downward arc of the same width. This creates the bottom of a shell that interlocks with the first. Continue alternating up and down arcs across the entire surface. Work as quickly as you can maintain even arcs; hesitation shows as a kink in the shell curve.
The finished pattern should show evenly spaced scallops covering the full tray.
Printing and cleaning
Hold the alum-mordanted sheet by two diagonal corners and lower it gently onto the size, avoiding air pockets. Allow contact for 20 to 30 seconds.
Lift the sheet from one corner. The pattern transfers to the sheet. Rinse under a gentle stream of cold water to remove the carrageenan, then lay flat to dry.
Skim the residual colour from the size surface before laying a new print.
